text:
For those morons who think that illegal downloading and piracy is a victimless crime and hurts no one, did you know that Nintendo of North America, my favorite game franchise, almost went bankrupt because of video game piracy? 
 So even though 99% of the time, those who download illegally usually don't get caught, is this really a "victimless" crime.
Its mostly those who distribute or sell pirated software on the web that get caught and arrested by the FBI.
Besides most of the games that are illegally downloaded and used on emulators are not made to be played on a computer. Just imagine playing a Switch game on a PC? 
I am in NO WAY against emulators as long as its used to play a legitimate game cart or disc. 
If everyone who downloads or gets a game illegally thinks that its just a copy "only for me"  then believe me, piracy is a big issue.

author: @ZenSymphonia

text: Selling is worse by a long shot, think of it this way, if you simply pirate something for your own use, to a company that's just one sale lost (and even if they don't like it most companies usually won't bother taking action, a general rule is the older something you pirate is the chances are less likely that a company will decide to take action) and as such doesn't affect a company's bottom line nearly as much, if you sell pirated material on the other hand (especially in large quantities) you're essentially profiting off of someone else's work without the original company getting any of that money, this means for every copy you sell that's another possible game sale that a company could lose and losses from such sales of pirated games can add up pretty quickly and affect a company's bottom line much more (some might argue that selling something that's pre-owned also takes away from a company's bottom line since they don't see any money from such sales and looking at it from that perspective that is true, however the difference with selling something pre-owned is the person selling the pre-owned item is a lawful owner of the official product and as such said owner has a right to do with the product as they see fit since that official copy is their property, whereas pirated copies are not official in any capacity and the ones selling them have no right to do so).

text: Aren't there far bigger issues out there worthy of sending in SWAT Teams and shuffling through the court system than someone who happens to download a tv show/movie/or game?  Just seems to me like a waste of resources.  And in all honesty, Piracy is the hydra that can never be destroyed.  Look at every single time that industry has tried to curtail the copying of intellectual property.  In the VHS Days, studios saw the recording of movies and TV shows has the devil.  They lost that one.  When the internet came and sites like napster emerged, they first tried shutting down the services, suing downloaders, threatening ISPs, and even lobbying for government regulations with SOPA.....and each time copying prevailed.  Hell, you shut down piratebay and now it is back up without all of the central servers used previously.  Perhaps if the companies treated their customers like customers and not like criminals, we would all have more respect.  But as long as the internet exists, piracy will never go away.....cut off one head and two shall take its place....Hail Hydra.

text:
Laominos To summarize and agree with your point, people who pirate were never going to buy the thing in the first place, so it's not a loss in revenue to the copyright holder.  If it weren't available for free download, it simply would not be downloaded nor purchased, the person pirating the movie, for example would wait until it was on regular TV before paying for a DVD.   

If a person pirates a move and they love the movie, two things are more likely to happen which benefit the copyright holder, firstly, the person who saw it will talk it up as a good movie to other people, some of which may actually purchase the thing to see for themselves.  Secondly, seeing that the movie doesn't suck might just encourage them to buy the DVD.  

I know a lot of people who will pirate a movie, like it, then go buy it, same for music as they want to support that which they like.  But if a movie sucks, it ends up in their trash bin and life goes on.  even for music they may end up keeping the free MP3's they illegally downloaded but they'll buy a T-shirt/Hat or concert tickets for an artist they like based on that illegal download. 

In a way making people pay for their media before seeing if they'll like it at all is putting the consumer in a box where they have to pay 15 bucks for a DVD of a movie that ends up being complete shit and then they regret the purchase... this makes people NOT buy because it's a hit or miss system.
author: @verisimilitudeteller

text:
johnsonfromwisconsin
No, it's perfectly logical. Do you think people pirate because they're just sitting on money which they're just not going to spend, or that they didn't have money for the media in the first place?   The logical slip isn't mine, it's yours, people aren't going to otherwise spend that which they don't have.  Most pirating is done by poor people not rich people.

Let's say someone has 20 bucks of surplus cash to spend which works out to like 2 DVD's or something, so they pirate 20 movies, pick their two favourites and either buy the DVD's to get the extra material on them, or as in the case of music, get a poster or T-shirt or other "gear" or go to a concert, which then promotes/supports the film/album/artist etc.?

Happens all the time, and if you ask any studio to tell you where they make more money, box office or merchandising, they'll likely point to Star Wars as the golden child example of where that bread is buttered.

No, but we all have choices to make with the money we have.  If we can steal music or movies, we get to spend on other things.  The logical slip is definately all yours.  Rich or poor, if piracy were impossible it would alter how people spend to the IP holder's benefit.

author: @AllYourBaseRBelong2Us

text:
Let's look at actual data instead of thought experiments.  https://www.engadget.com/2017/09/22/eu-suppressed-study-piracy-no-sales-impact/
Johnson, you're wrong.  Except in the case of recently released blockbuster movies, piracy has no negative impact on sales.  In some cases (particularly multiplayer games designed to work better with larger populations of players) piracy increases sales.
In general, a pirated copy does not represent a lost sale. It represents an additional set of eyeballs.
That said: buying and selling pirated copies is a whole different story.  If someone is willing to purchase a copy of a work, that money should go to the creator of the work.  But if someone isn't willing to purchase a copy of a work, nobody is hurt if they get to experience it anyway.

I hound you because you are pretentious.

First and foremost, youtube is entirely proprietary. View the page source, they do not use any external libraries, only proprietary google libraries. Even if you use an ad blocker, you are giving "the evil closed source man" page views.

In addition, Windows does provide full control over nearly all aspects of the operating system through a large set of well documented APIs. If you cannot program, I suppose that's your problem.

In short, I use linux on my servers. It's a decent platform for my purposes. That being said, I don't go around on youtube videos shouting about it.

text:
*On that note, though, the website GoG.com (Good Old Games) sells some really old games, some of which even require DOSBox to run them (like Rayman). So even then, you can still get the old games legally. Albeit, sans the disc(s), manual, and fat jewel cases.

I do technically pirate things like cartridge-based games, *but* I *also* usually only play the ROMs briefly — just long enough to figure out if I like the game. If I don't, I delete the ROM; if I DO — heck YES I'm going to buy the game.
author: @KizulEmeraldfire

text:
Mondello's mistake: SELLING the illegally-copied things.

Not that I actually advocate the distribution of cracked share-/nag-ware software for free — unless it's years and years old (id est: abandonware. Such as something like Adobe Photoshop 7.0.1., or a game made for DOS*), and the original company isn't selling or even SUPPORTING the software anymore.

You can't lose money on something that you have effectively — and, basically, WILLINGLY — thrown away.

text:
Dinkers is not a moron. You are.

American Home Recording Act of 1992 DOES allow you to give a CD away, with some VERY high exceptions:

I. It must be on a Audio CD-R Disc, which cost 2-5 times more than a regular blank disc.

II. You must record the songs using a "Digital Audio Recording Device." 
COMPUTERS DO NOT COUNT! If you made the CD with a Burner on your Computer, you broke the law!

Also, if you made a copy of "Toy Story" that didn't include a "Digital Copy" then yes, you broke the law.

text:
I've heard a lot of piracy is caused by increasingly restricted DRM. Let's say your main gaming computer doesn't have internet access, but you have another (less powerful) computer that does. You can't play a game you've bought on either computer, because one can't go online to activate it, and the other can't handle the system requirements. So what do people do? They get a cracked copy that can work without online activation.
When it's more convenient to pirate than to buy, there's a problem.

text:
Justify why you have a moral right to protect your intangibles.

The justification most people use is "i thought of it, it's mine". This is flawed thinking. Hypothetically, suppose you came up with a novel business model. Do you have a right to insist that you get a monopoly on this idea? This has the same validity as copyright.

You have no moral right to tell people what to do when it doesn't physically affect you or your property. (intellectual "property" isn't property under US law)

text: Here's the thing.  If I record a CD and publish it, I want each and individual person to buy one copy of that CD.  If one kid buys said CD and copies it for ten of his friends, that's fine.  If a million kids do that, that's 9 million CDs I didn't sell, and 10 people who are enjoying my music without giving anything up for it.  And that is a crime in and of itself.  Yes, I have a moral right to protect what I create.  If I made a company, I would make sure to at least charge for the products.

text:
Our creative department has confirmed the H does map to the english 'V' sound at 2:51. But the last letter that looks like an L (after the H) is meaningless.

The time for this research will be added to our bill.
author: @DiggityCheckIt

text:
We do not know why DP chose to use Klingons. 

A check against the official Klingon dictionary appears to confirm the letters are meaningless so no copyright claim can be made (there is no letter that looks like an H in Klingon).

But we reserve the right to protect our characters and likenesses and have filed papers to have this video removed or comply with our royalty policies.

Dear ChromeBallz,

Many E-publishers don't pay an advance on royalties. The author gets is a % of  sales.

Print publishers advances (money paid on signing contract, after which nothing is paid to the author for 18 months) begin at  $1,000 to $5,000.

You think an e-book is cheap to do? Writing takes the same (months!). Editing... days. Models still pose. Photogs. Artists paint. ISBNs and copyright cost the same. Distribution still costs. The savings are only in paper, ink, storage.

author: @linkrazr

text:
In addition I doubt the use of Securom was even intended to prevent piracy.

EA is not dumb, they know how ineffective Securom was by its application in previous titles. However Securom is very effective at something, regardless of whether or not it was pirated.

Resale prevention. 

You cannot resell Securom games, which allows EA to keep the sale price artificially high because customers cannot buy second hand.

Even so, this could have been done with less draconian DRM like account tying.
author: @AnnoyedDragon

text:
Spore is one of the most pirated games in history, it was pirated long before it ever hit shop shelves. Securom did nothing to prevent the game from being pirated; and yet it was still pushed onto legitimate customers.

Piracy is EA's problem, not mine. I did nothing to "had it coming" considering I went out and -bought- the game as soon as it came out.

If they want to protect their games then fine, but use a method that isn't as idiotic as typing activations to variable hardware.

This is what backups should be for.
